postData.contentHtml:
Pope Leo has made a powerful statement on the ethical implications of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in his inaugural theological document, "Magnifica Humanitas" (Magnificent Humanity). Released amidst growing global concerns, the encyclical serves as a comprehensive warning, highlighting AI's potential to propagate misinformation, escalate conflict, and lead the world down a perilous path of unending war.

At the heart of Pope Leo's message is an urgent appeal for AI Regulation. He implores governments to "slow down" AI development and establish stringent regulations, independent oversight, and educated user practices. "What is needed is a more active political involvement that is capable of slowing things down when everything is accelerating," stated Leo, emphasizing the necessity of proactive political engagement to manage the rapid advancements in AI.

A significant portion of his address focuses on AI in Warfare. The Pontiff voiced profound apprehension concerning autonomous weapons systems that have progressed "practically beyond any human reach to govern them." He asserted that entrusting lethal decisions to AI systems is "not permissible" and mandated that any deployment of AI in warfare adhere to the strictest ethical boundaries. This stance is further bolstered by his notable Rejection of the "Just War" Theory. In a historic departure from centuries of Church doctrine, Pope Leo explicitly disavowed the theory, stating it has been "all too often used to justify any kind of war" and is now "outdated." He contends that the application of force, violence, and weaponry signifies a "relational poverty" with dire repercussions for civilian populations.

Beyond conflict, the encyclical addresses the profound societal impact of AI, particularly concerning Labor and "New Forms of Slavery." Pope Leo underscored ethical issues regarding AI data ownership, arguing against its sole possession by private entities. He condemned "new forms of slavery" experienced by individuals involved in maintaining AI systems and manufacturing AI-dependent technological devices. He specifically cited the hazardous work of children and adolescents extracting rare earth elements, lamenting that "The bodies of these people are scarred, injured and worn down so that computational flow may continue uninterruptedly." This reality, he states, "deeply challenges the moral conscience of our time."

In a moment of historic acknowledgment, Pope Leo also offered a personal and sincere Apology for the Church's Role in Slavery, regretting the Catholic Church's belated condemnation of transatlantic slavery until the 19th century, labeling it a "wound in Christian memory."

The Pope concluded with a compelling Call for Unified Action, invoking the biblical story of the Tower of Babel. He implored leaders and all individuals of good faith to abandon the construction of "yet another Tower of Babel" and instead "join forces in building up the common good." He encouraged society not to abandon efforts to address the risks posed by AI, reminding everyone that while power to make a difference may vary, "no one is without responsibility. We all have our own areas for action."

Simulated social sentiment surrounding "Pope Leo AI" has been largely positive, reflecting a global resonance with his call for ethical consideration and responsible governance in the age of artificial intelligence. His encyclical is poised to spark critical discussions and inspire action across religious, political, and technological spheres.
postData.title: Pope Leo's 'Magnifica Humanitas': A Groundbreaking Call for AI Regulation and Peace
postData.summary: In his first major theological document, Pope Leo issues a profound warning about the unchecked development of Artificial Intelligence, calling for global regulation, an end to 'just war' theory, and a unified approach to ensure AI serves humanity's common good.

sources.title: Pope Leo warns of AI fueling warfare in first major theological document
Pope urges AI regulation, apologizes for Church's role in slavery
